press_release_title: Chronic total occlusion treatment in Hyderabad
press_abstract: Chronic total occlusion treatment in Hyderabad is more complicated than a standard coronary angioplasty, explains Dr. Sudheer Koganti, MD, an interventional cardiologist at Citizens Hospital. The artery's route is frequently concealed because it is entirely obstructed. Typically, two catheters are inserted: one to clarify the path of the artery forward and the other to demonstrate collateral flow from other vessels to the affected location. Special methods, mainly the implantation of stents, are used to reconnect these two locations. Sometimes more than one surgery is required to provide the best long-term results. A total occlusion occurs when a coronary artery gets entirely blocked rather than simply constricted. If a complete blockage lasts three months or more, it is referred to as "chronic total occlusion," or CTO for short. CTO patients may have chest pain, shortness of breath, and fatigue, as well as an elevated risk of heart attack and other significant heart diseases. Symptoms of chronic total occlusion include chest pain, shortness of breath, and feeling tired. The risk factors for chronic total occlusion include smoking, high blood pressure, diabetes, obesity, high blood cholesterol, having coronary artery disease or a previous heart attack, and having a family history of premature coronary artery disease. The chronic total occlusion treatment in Hyderabad is determined by the severity of the disease and its symptoms. A variety of medications may be used to decrease symptoms, halt or stop arterial plaque development, and ease heart work. Antiplatelet medicines, beta-blockers, ACE inhibitors, angiotensin receptor blockers (ARBs), calcium channel blockers, statins, and nitrates are examples of pharmaceuticals. All of these drugs are intended to alleviate symptoms and enhance the quality of life. If the limits and symptoms persist, additional therapies may be required. The chronic total occlusion treatment in Hyderabad includes PCI, which stands for percutaneous coronary intervention. Dr. Sudheer Koganti, an interventional cardiologist, inserts a tiny tube called a sheath (similar to an IV) into a blood vessel in the wrist or groyne during this minimally invasive technique. Following that, a catheter (a smaller tube) is inserted into the sheath and guided to the heart and, finally, the damaged coronary artery. A tiny guidewire is then inserted through the catheter and across the constricted or obstructed segment of the artery by the interventional cardiologist. This is followed by the insertion of a second, smaller catheter, which is outfitted with a balloon that inflates when it reaches the restricted or blocked segment of the artery. The balloon opens the artery, allowing blood to flow back to the heart. A stent, which is a mesh tube, may be inserted into the freshly opened part of the blood vessel by the doctor. The stent keeps the artery open and allows blood to flow through it. CABG stands for coronary artery bypass grafting. CABG, or coronary artery bypass surgery, is part of chronic total occlusion treatment in Hyderabad and involves a surgeon grafting one end of a healthy vein or artery obtained from elsewhere in the body to the aorta and the other end to the damaged coronary artery at a location past the obstruction. The blockage in the coronary artery persists, but the newly connected blood channel reroutes flow around it, bypassing it. Typically, CABG is performed by opening the chest through the sternum (breastbone). The chronic total occlusion treatment in Hyderabad comprises numerous components. The first goal is to improve and, eventually, eliminate lifestyle-limiting angina symptoms. The second goal is to achieve a long-term result; the third is to guarantee that specific CAD risk variables are well controlled, with the goal of reducing the risk of future CAD-related disorders. In addition to consulting a specialist for treatment, people with CTO should undertake heart-healthy lifestyle changes such as stopping smoking, exercising, controlling blood pressure and cholesterol levels, and maintaining a healthy weight. "At Citizens Hospital, our Complex Coronary Intervention Programme focuses on the chronic total occlusion treatment in Hyderabad of patients with severe coronary artery disease and, in particular, patients with CTO," explains Citizens Hospital interventional cardiologist Dr. Sudheer Koganti, MBBS, MD, FRCM. "Our multidisciplinary team examines each case in depth and collaborates to determine the best treatment option for each patient." We have doctors who have received specialised training in the performance of CTO PCI in order to provide the best possible outcomes for our patients. We also carry out these treatments with the help of two committed and experienced operators to ensure that the procedure is as safe and efficient as possible."

press_release_title: Dr. Sudheer Koganti is one the Best Doctor for TAVI/TAVR in Hyderabad
press_abstract: he best doctor for TAVI/TAVR in Hyderabad is Dr. Sudheer Koganti. He has successfully conducted thousands of heart disease surgeries. Dr. Sudheer Koganti has done complex aortic surgeries, bypasses, open heart surgeries, and many more. Transcatheter aortic valve replacement (TAVR) is a procedure in which doctors use a man-made valve to replace a damaged aortic valve. This surgery is also referred to as open-heart surgery. Aortic valves manage blood flow in our hearts; therefore, if the valve becomes stiff, you have a condition known as aortic stenosis. If blood flow through the valve becomes uncontrollable, you may have heart failure. Dr. Sudheer Koganti is the best doctor for TAVI/TAVR in Hyderabad as well as a heart valve expert. TAVR involves surgeons inserting a blood vessel in your leg to deliver and install an artificial valve in your heart. TAVI (transcatheter aortic valve implantation) is another name for TAVR (transcatheter aortic valve replacement). If you have symptoms of heart failure due to aortic valve stenosis, you may require TAVR. It also raises the possibility of typical open-heart aortic valve replacement surgery due to factors such as advancing age, heart condition, stroke history, illness of the liver, kidney failure, diabetes, and previous radiation treatment for lung cancer. The most common TAVR risks are bleeding, heart attack, failure of the kidneys, replacement valve leakage, heart failure, stroke caused by a decrease in blood flow to the brain, and damaged blood vessels. Other hazards of TAVR may exist, depending on your medical condition. So, before you undergo the TAVR treatment, make sure to discuss everything with your best doctor for TAVI/TAVR in Hyderabad. Before surgery, be sure to discuss your health with your doctors so that your medical and surgical teams may assess your entire health. This may involve x-rays, CT scans, blood tests, MRIs, or any other testing in this process. Stop taking any medications that your doctor has forbidden you to use. Quit smoking and any other alcohol consumption. Before surgery, avoid eating and drinking, especially after midnight. Before undergoing TAVR, you must complete the following tasks: Remove any jewellery you are wearing during TAVR, as well as any other object that may interfere with the operation. You must empty your bladder before to the surgery. Remove your clothes and put on the gown that was supplied to you. During the process, your medical team analyses your blood pressure, blood sugar level, heart function, and heart rhythm and manages any therapy you require. Once the new valve is properly fixed, your surgeon will replace the old one. After this procedure, your health will be monitored by the medical team, and you may spend some nights in the hospital recovering. press_release_title: Women need more sleep than men
press_abstract: HYDERABAD: Not many people are aware that women need more sleep than men, said doctors from the city. According to an internationally renowned doctor, women require 8-10 hours of sleep each night, while men can get by with 6-7- 8 hours of sleep. Although the majority of city doctors concur, few have stated that the amount of sleep needed depends on the body type and health of each individual. However, they concur that due to hormonal differences, women sleep more than men do. Also Read - Global Temperatures Stuck at Near-Record Highs in April: EU Monitor Advertisement Women require more sleep in order to create certain hormone reserves, said the doctor, adding, “Men can get away with 7-8 hours of sleep, sometimes even six hours, but women need 8-10 hours of uninterrupted sleep,” he said. He went on to say that this is because women rely heavily on their adrenals to produce daily hormones, whereas men do not, as they can produce 2-3% of their adrenals in 98 percent which is produced by testicles. However, he claimed that women require specific adrenal or hormone production to create their entire anabolic state, he said. Also Read - Astroguide for May 8, 2025 City gynecologists and endocrinologists agree that women require more sleep than men due to hormonal differences and women’s greater responsibilities. If a woman doesn’t get enough sleep, which is close to eight hours and more, then she is likely to have hormonal imbalances, they said. Women release certain hormones during their deep sleep which helps them get energized and active. According to Dr K Shilpi Reddy, Clinical director for women and children at a well-known city hospital, if a woman's sleep is disrupted and shorter, she will experience several problems such as irregular menstrual cycle, and disruption in growth hormones. “It is more about good quality sleep and the requirements differ from person to person. But women need more sleep than men. However, they sleep less even when their body demands more sleep than men,” added Dr Reddy. Also Read - Beauty Lies in What You Eat However, endocrinologists in the city asserted that women do indeed require more sleep than men, albeit only a small amount more—just 10-15 minutes. Men and women are advised to get eight hours of sleep each night, but because of gender and hormonal differences, women require an additional 15 minutes. “Men typically sleep more soundly and uninterruptedly than women. Men's sexual functions are impacted if they don't get the recommended amount of sleep. Insufficient sleep can also have an impact on reproductive, sexual, and metabolic processes. Women do need an extra 10 minutes of sleep because they experience more sleep disorders than men do,” said Dr. Sandeep, an endocrinologist.

press_release_title: Proactive For Her Expands Clinic Locations and Adds Fertility Service
press_abstract: BANGALORE, India, April 10, 2024 /PRNewswire/ -- Proactive For Her, the leading omnichannel women's health clinic in India, is excited to announce the opening of three more clinic locations—Whitefield, New BEL Rd and JP Nagar—in Bangalore. These clinics will allow Proactive For Her to expand their medical care to over 500,000 women across Bangalore. Additionally, in order to serve their loyal customers more deeply, Proactive has enhanced their fertility care offerings across its clinics and online throughout India as well. Since its founding in 2020, Proactive For Her has revolutionised the healthcare experience for women in India and positioned itself as a leader for world-class care that addresses evolving lifestyle choices. Understanding that a woman's health is affected by the surrounding culture and society, Proactive For Her's model is unique in that they practice evidence-based, culturally responsive care with a focus on preventative healthcare, across life stages. Proactive For Her's medical team, other than being experts in field, are also trained to be trauma-informed, body positive, sex positive, non-judgemental and queer affirmative. Proactive For Her has grown significantly since their first in-person clinic opened in 2022, having experienced a 144% increase in new patient visits. The company now operates seven locations in Bangalore's prime areas of Indiranagar, Koramangala, HSR Layout, Kalyan Nagar, with the newest three locations in New BEL Rd, JP Nagar and Whitefield, making the care women deserve easily accessible. The company plans to open 120 locations in the next five years. "As with all of our clinics, our mission with our three newest locations in Bangalore is to make it easier for women to access our excellent, culturally responsive, non-judgemental healthcare. We are always looking for ways to improve how we serve our loyal customers," says Achitha Jacob, founder and CEO of Proactive For Her. "Thus we extend vaccinations, our diagnostics, procedures and cancer screening, in addition to our in-depth doctor consultations that our customers value, in all of our clinic locations." In order to better serve the needs of women, Proactive For Her has also increased its fertility offerings. Between rising infertility rates to couples prioritising family planning and later pregnancies, the demand for fertility services is surging and Proactive For Her provides various early stage interventions. "We're constantly looking at the clinical need in our patient population to determine where we go deeper. Fertility has been an area we've invested in with new services. IVF is not the only answer. Many women can benefit from trying less invasive options first," says Dr. Geeta Aurangabadkar, Clinical Director of Proactive For Her. To schedule an appointment at one of their clinics or virtually, please visit https://proactiveforher.com/schedule/. To learn more about Proactive For Her's services, visit https://proactiveforher.com. About Proactive For Her: Proactive For Her is India's leading omnichannel women's health clinic offering judgement-free care from puberty to menopause for women's evolving lifestyle choices. Proactive For Her addresses menstrual health, sexual health, PCOS, painful sex, fertility, mental health, in addition to cancer prevention, pregnancy and postpartum services such as lactation consulting and pelvic floor physiotherapy. As a one-stop women's healthcare provider, Proactive For Her blends in-person medical consultations with lab tests, scans and procedures in warm, nurturing, safe spaces designed to make it easier for women to experience the care they deserve all under one roof. With a focus on preventative health, Proactive For her spreads awareness about annual check-ups with pap smears and cancer screening, cervical cancer (HPV) vaccination, STI/STD screening. By empowering women to book their first gynaecology consultation, Proactive For Her engages with women early on so they are empowered to take charge of their health journey.

press_release_title: Diabetes type 2 symptoms: Doctor points out nine 'early warning signs' of high blood sugar
press_abstract: Dr Geeta Aurangabadkar – who sits on the board for the international journal of diabetes and endocrinology – explained the condition in great detail, and provided her expert advice. "Diabetes is a disease that occurs when your blood glucose level is abnormally high," she began. "Blood glucose from the food we eat is our main energy source. The pancreas, with the help of the hormone insulin, helps glucose get absorbed by cells for energy. "Sometimes, your body doesn’t make enough insulin or even no insulin at all. Your body may be unable to use insulin in other cases. "Glucose then stays in your blood and doesn’t reach your cells," she explained. Dr Aurangabadkar pointed out that type 2 diabetes can develop at any age. It begins when insulin resistance takes place, and one of the "early warning signs" is how frequently you use the loo.

press_release_title: SMIH doctors conduct 2 TAVR procedures in a day
press_abstract: The doctors of the Cardiology Department of Shri Mahant Indiresh Hospital have conducted two TAVR procedures on two patients in a single day. This is an outstanding achievement because TAVR technique is an ultramodern heart procedure technique for heart patients. The TAVR technique is being used in just a few renowned medical colleges in India and abroad. Shri Mahant Indiresh Hospital is one of these colleges. Till now, a dozen TAVR procedures have been done at Shri Mahant Indiresh Hospital in the past 4 months. Senior Cardiologist and Director, Cardiac Sciences, Shri Mahant Indiresh Hospital, Dr Salil Garg shared significant information related to modern Cardiology. At the Cath Lab of Shri Mahant Indiresh Hospital, TAVR and other significant procedures are being done successfully. This is the reason that heart patients from North India and other states are reaching Shri Mahant Indiresh Hospital for ultra modern heart procedures. Coordination between the doctors of Cardiology and CTVS Department of Shri Mahant Indiresh Hospital has made possible complete heart treatment under one roof. TAVR Technique has made heart treatment smooth for the cardiologists. TAVR Technique treatment is available at Shri Mahant Indiresh Hospital under CGHS, ECHS, Golden Card and ESI schemes also. The beneficiaries of these these schemes are taking the benefit of TAVR Technique at Shri Mahant Indiresh Hospital. In TAVR Technique, the valve is replaced without any incision. Under this procedure, the patient is discharged from the hospital within 4 days. Before this technique, the valve was replaced by incision and the patient needed to be admitted in the hospital for as much as 15 days. progress. HOD, Department of Cardiology, Shri Mahant Indiresh Hospital, Dr Sahil Mahajan stated that TAVR (Trans Catheter Aortic Valve Replacement) is sort of a lifeline for the heart patients. At Shri Mahant Indiresh Hospital, 2 Cath Labs equipped with ultra modern facilities and team of efficient cardiologists are available.

press_release_title: Successful Treatment Of Heart Patients At Shri Mahant Indiresh Hospital By B.M.V. Technique
press_abstract: The doctors of Cardiology Deptt. of Shri Mahant Indiresh Hospital have treated a 26 years old pregnant woman suffering from Rheumatic Heart Disease and a 16 years old female’s shrunk valve by Balloon Mitral Valvuloplasty (B.M.V. technique) Priyanka, a 26 years old pregnant woman, suffering from Rheumatic Heart Disease, got her valve repaired by Open Heart Surgery. But her valve got shrunk again after a few years. Many hospitals advised her to go to big hospitals of metro cities for getting treated again. Then the patient reached Shri Mahant Indiresh Hopsital, Dehradun and was seen by Cardiology Deptt.’s Assistant Professor Dr. Abhishek Mittal. Dr. Abhishek Mittal along with senior Professor and HOD, Deptt. of Cardiology, Dr. Sahil Mahajan studied the report of the patient. They decided to treat the patient by Balloon Mitral Valvuloplasty (B.M.V.) technique. Professor Dr. Sahil Mahajan, Dr. Abhishek Mittal and their team conducted the successful treatment of the patient by B.M.V. technique. Dr. Sahil Mahajan informed that the case of this pregnant woman was a very complicated and rare one. He said that in year 2017 also an effort was made to treat the patient through Balloon Mitral Valvuloplasty but it could not succeed because at that time because the valve became defective. That time, woman’s open heart surgery was done through which the valve was repaired but it got shrunk after a few years. While in the other case the 16 years old female named Reena Kumari reached the OPD of Cardiology Deptt., Shri Mahant Indiresh Hospital to be seen by Dr. Abhishek Mittal. Dr. Abhishek Mittal after investigations diagnosed that the female suffered from Rheumatic Heart Disease and her Mitral Valve had shrunk whose treatment was possible through Balloon Mitral Valvuloplasty technique. The treatment of this patient was also done successfully by Professor & HOD, Deptt. of Cardiology, Dr. Sahil Mahajan and Cardiologist Dr. Abhishek Mittal. In this technique, by means of catheter the balloon is made to reach so as to expand the valve. Balloon Mitral Valvuloplasty (B.M.V.) is one such procedure in which the heart valve is expanded thus improving the heart bold flow. Heart Valve controls the flow of blood through the heart. As compared to Open Heart Surgery, Balloon Mitral Valvuloplasty (B.M.V.) is preferred technique than Open Heart Surgery whose benefits are as under: Fast treatment Minor spots Less complication Less painful

press_release_title: Ozempic in focus after rumours surrounding Karan Johar: Risks of weight-loss drug
press_abstract: Filmmaker Karan Johar recently found himself at the centre of a controversy over his dramatic weight loss. Speculation arose that he had used Ozempic, a diabetes medication known for its weight-loss side effects, to achieve the transformation. The rumours gained traction after Maheep Kapoor, on the show "Fabulous Lives of Bollywood Wives," criticised people for using Ozempic for weight loss, causing shortages for diabetic patients. However, Johar, in an Instagram story, clarified that his weight loss was the result of "being healthy and eating well". The incident has sparked a broader discussion about the weight loss methods and the ethical use of prescription drugs for cosmetic purposes, most notably Ozempic. We look at what this drug actually is and how its use has transformed over time.

press_release_title: Nothing sweet in sex life? Blame it on sugar
press_abstract: Representative Image NAGPUR: Diabetes, a growing concern in India with rising cases of early onset, can throw a wrench into your sex life, no matter your gender. A recent discussion in Nagpur between leading sexologists and diabetes experts tackled this sensitive topic, highlighting the connection between poorly managed blood sugar and a decline in sexual function for both men and women. The discussion was held as part of the 'Hello Diabetes Academia (HDA) 2024: An International Conclave'. Six doctors across the country spoke on six sex-ated subjects in context of diabetes. Dr Sanjay Deshpande, a renowned sexologist, highlighted the link between diabetes and male sexual health. "It's well-known that diabetes can decrease sexual desire and function. Both diabetes and a decrease in male sex hormones, sometimes referred to as 'male menopause', can start around 40 years of age. This can lead to diabetic men losing sexual interest and enjoyment earlier in life," he said. Dr Punit Saxena discussed evaluating erectile dysfunction in diabetic men. He explained that a proper evaluation for erectile dysfunction in diabetic men involves a combination of physical exams, blood tests, and potentially a psychological assessment to determine the underlying cause. Dr Saurabh Mishra explained psychogenic erectile dysfunction in diabetic patients. "Psychogenic erectile dysfunction happens when emotional or mental factors like stress or anxiety make it difficult to get or maintain an erection, even if there's no physical problem. Diabetes can contribute to these mental stresses and worsen the issue," he said. Dr Lotika Purohit addressed female sexual dysfunction and diabetes. "Women with diabetes are more prone to sexual problems due to nerve damage and blood flow issues. This can lead to vaginal dryness, making sex uncomfortable, decreased libido due to hormonal changes, and difficulty achieving orgasm. These issues can significantly impact a woman's sexual life," she said. Dr Deepak Jumani, a renowned Mumbai sexologist, concluded the discussion by talking about treatment options. "It's very common for diabetic patients to experience decreased sexual desire," Dr Jumani said. "There are effective treatments available, but you need to be open up about the problem and get checked by your doctor. There are both medical and surgical options to address erectile dysfunction," he said. Dr Sunil Gupta highlighted a crucial point. "As many as 40% of diabetic patients, both men and women, suffer from sexual problems, but they hesitate to discuss it with their doctor. If you discuss it with your diabetologist, you can get a solution for sure," he said.

press_release_title: Dengue patient in UP dies after allegedly being transfused fruit juice instead of platelets, hospital sealed
press_abstract: A dengue patient in Uttar Pradesh passed away after allegedly being transfused fruit juice instead of blood platelets, as per media reports. The police said that the hospital has been sealed. After a video of the alleged incident went viral on social media, the district administration swung into action and the hospital was sealed on Thursday on the direction of Uttar Pradesh Deputy Chief Minister Brajesh Pathak. The patient, Pradeep Pandey, was shifted to another hospital, where he died after his condition deteriorated, officials said. However, no FIR has been registered at the local police sta .. The owner of the private hospital claimed that the platelets were brought from a different medical facility and the patient had a reaction after three units were transfused. In a tweet, Deputy CM Pathak said, "Taking cognisance of the viral video at the hospital where a dengue patient was transfused with sweet lemon juice instead of platelets, on my directive the hospital was sealed and the platelet packets have been sent for testing." "If found guilty, strict action will be taken against the hospital," he said. According to sources, the patient died due to the transfusion of "wrong platelets" and the samples of those will be examined. Asked about the reason for sealing the hospital, an officer, on condition of anonymity, said it has been done on the instructions of the chief medical officer and will remain so till the sample is tested. Saurabh Mishra, the owner of the hospital, said that since the p .. "They brought five units of platelets from SRN Hospital. After transfusion of three units, the patient had a reaction. So we stopped it," PTI quoted him as saying. Mishra said that the platelets should be examined and their origin should be traced because they had the sticker of SRN hospital on them. District Magistrate Sanjay Kumar Khatri said, "An inquiry is underway and the platelets will also be tested."

press_release_title: Kailash Hospital, Greater Noida does medical miracle by treating rare heart disease
press_abstract: Kailash Multi-speciality Hospital’s Cardiology department has once again proved its excellence in the field of medical care by successfully treating a rare heart disease. “We are really proud of Dr Sujeet Narain. All the doctors of our hospital have congratulated him for his commendable work. We will also feature him in our hospital’s magazine. We are really grateful to have him as a part of our team,” said Dinesh Sharma, Director, Kailash Hospital, Greater Noida. Diagnosed in patient named Preeti Chauhan, who is currently below 40 years of age, the treatment happened under the very experienced cardiologist, Dr Sujeet Narain. He came across the case in September 2021, when the patient was experiencing breathlessness and irregular heartbeats. It was found that the patient was suffering from Dilated Cardiomyopathy which weakens the heart muscles affecting the function of the heart. “The situation grew severe when Preeti had fallen unconscious. That is when we realised that immediate medical intervention was required. It was definitely a costly affair,” said Bhupesh Chauhan, patient’s husband. After a few months of the treatment, in December 2021, the patient was again diagnosed with Ventricular Tachycardia, where the patient is at the risk of a sudden cardiac death. Following the diagnosis, another heart disease, Sarcoidosis was also suspected after a cardiac MRI. But all this was treated successfully by putting best medical practices to use. “We have successfully planted a dual-chamber Intra-cardiac Defibrillator, now the patient has fully-recovered,” said Dr Narain, Cardiologist, Kailash Hospital, Greater Noida. It is quite difficult to treat Ventricular Tachycardia. There were some drugs that were not suitable for the patient, as a result we had to plant an ICD (Implantable Cardioverter Defibrillator) and administer drugs that successfully treated the disease,” added the doctor. “Dr Sujeet is a great personality. We did not require a second opinion as the treatment was effective and the CCU team was very prompt in responding to the emergency situation,” Bhupesh told us. Given severity of the issue and seriousness of medication, the patient, was allowed limited family interaction, however, the hospital staff kept her feel at home. “They would cheer me up and boost my morale whenever I felt sad or isolated,” said Preeti. “The patient has now fully-recovered and been discharged from the hospital. We hope she has a happy and disease free life ahead,” stated B. K. Sharma, AGM, Kailash Hospital, Greater Noida.

press_release_title: Could lifestyle changes reverse the growing diabetes epidemic? Experts weigh in at TOI Medithon Part-6
press_abstract: Diabetes is a chronic condition that affects millions worldwide, characterized by high blood sugar levels due to the body's inability to produce or use insulin effectively. Raising awareness about diabetes is critical because it helps people recognize symptoms early. Early detection can significantly improve outcomes through timely interventions, lifestyle changes, and medical treatments. Education on diabetes empowers individuals to make healthier choices, such as eating a balanced diet, staying physically active, and managing stress. It also reduces stigma by fostering understanding and compassion for those living with the condition. With the global rise in diabetes cases, awareness campaigns are vital for promoting regular health check-ups and emphasizing prevention strategies. By increasing public knowledge and encouraging healthy habits, we can reduce the prevalence of diabetes and improve the quality of life for millions. Awareness isn't just about information-it's a step toward healthier, more informed communities and with this vision, TOI will organise the sixth medithon, under the campaign Live Well With Diabetes. The event will be held on January 30. Major topics around diabetes will be covered in the medithon in four different sessions where experts will discuss basics on diabetes and provide informative solutions to it. First session: Diabetes and Obesity The first session will focus on obesity, a silent epidemic and how it influences diabetes. The event will have the following experts in attendance: Dr. Om J Lakhani, MD, DNB Endocrinology. Consultant Endocrinologist, Zydus Hospital, Ahmedabad Dr. Harsh Durgia, MD, DM Endocrinology. Consultant Endocrinologist, Dr. Harsh's Endocrine & Diabetes Center, Rajkot Second session: Live well with Diabetes - Prevention, management and reversal Prevent diabetes by maintaining a healthy diet, exercising regularly, and managing weight. Manage it with balanced meals, regular monitoring, and medication as needed. In some cases, Type 2 diabetes can be reversed through sustained lifestyle changes like weight loss and improved fitness. The following experts will address the key queries in this session: Dr. Ghanshyam Agrawal MD, DM Endocrinology. Consultant Endocrinologist & Diabetologist, Lucknow Dr. Jayakrishnan C Menon, MD, DM (Endocrinology). Assistant Professor in Endocrinology, Jubilee Mission Medical College, Thrissur Dr. Kiran Kumar Pasam, MD, DM Endocrinology. Consultant Endocrinologist, AIG Hospitals, Hyderabad ??? Third session: Live Well with Diabetes: Getting to know about Diabetes Diabetes is a chronic condition where the body struggles to regulate blood sugar due to insufficient insulin or resistance to it. It comes in types: Type 1, Type 2, and gestational. Understanding its symptoms, risks, and management empowers individuals to lead healthier, more fulfilling lives despite the condition. Dr. Alka Gandhi, Consulting Diabetologist & Founder, Aayushi Advanced Diabetes Care Clinic, Mumbai Dr. Shefali Karkhanis, Consultant Diabetologist & Co-Director, Karkhanis Super-specialty Hospital, Thane Dr. Ami Sanghvi, Director & Consultant Diabetologist, Sanghvi Eye & Diabetes Care Centre, Mumbai Fourth session: Diabetes - Risk factors and complications Risk factors for diabetes include obesity, family history, sedentary lifestyle, poor diet, and age. Complications can arise if unmanaged, such as heart disease, kidney damage, nerve issues, vision loss, and slow wound healing. Early diagnosis and proper management are crucial to reducing these risks and maintaining overall health. Participate in the TOI Medithon Part-6 to understand the complications around diabetes as explained by the following experts: Dr. Jasjeet Singh Wasir, MD, Director, Endocrinology and Diabetes, Medanta, Gurugram Dr. Rohit Kapoor, MD, FACP, FACC, FRCP, Internal Medicine Director, Carewell Heart & Super Speciality Hospital, Amritsar Dr. Ashish Saxena, MD, FRCP, FIAE, PGDD, Consultant Internal Medicine, Diabetes & Non-Invasive Cardiology, Diabetes and Heart Centre & Delta Heart Centre, Ludhiana Awareness plays a crucial role in diabetes control by educating individuals about risk factors, symptoms, and the importance of early detection. It empowers people to make healthier lifestyle choices, adhere to medication regimens, and monitor blood sugar levels, ultimately reducing complications and improving quality of life.

press_release_title: Drugs not setting BP right? Go for renal denervation, says expert
press_abstract: Lucknow: For patients suffering from resistant hypertension, where blood pressure remains uncontrolled despite use of combination of three different medicines, experts say renal denervation therapy now offers an effective treatment option. Speaking at the 1st Mid-Term Heart India Conclave, Prof Akshyaya Pradhan from cardiology department at King George's Medical University on Sunday, said, "Hypertension affects human body in multiple ways-from causing stroke, heart diseases and kidney damage to even blindness. Many times, despite using multiple high-dose medicines, blood pressure remains uncontrolled. In such cases, renal denervation therapy, which was recently introduced at KGMU, provides a new ray of hope." He added that resistant hypertension accounted for about 10% of all hypertension cases, making it a significant challenge in the management of blood pressuredisorders. Explaining the procedure, Prof Pradhan said renal denervation therapy is a minimally invasive technique. A catheter is inserted into arteries leading to kidneys and radiofrequency energy or ultrasound waves are used to disrupt the nerves in the kidney arteries that contribute to high blood pressure. "By targeting these overactive nerves, blood pressure can be lowered significantly without relying solely on medications," he added. He also said that cost of renal denervation therapy is approximately Rs 3 to 4 lakh, depending on the case and hospital facilities. Adding to the discussion, Prof Pravesh Vishwakarma, faculty at the department of cardiology, KGMU, said, "Heart-related problems are number one cause of death not only in India but across the world." He explained that factors such as unhealthy diet, sedentary lifestyle, smoking, rising obesity, uncontrolled diabetes and stress were major contributors to the growing burden of heart diseases. "The solution lies in a holistic approach early screening, strict blood pressure control, cholesterol management, promoting exercise, healthy eating, and when necessary, applying advanced treatments like renal denervation therapy," emphasised Dr Alok Kumar Singh, Cardiologist from Varanasi. The conclave brought together around 300 doctors from across the country, focusing on raising awareness about new-age cardiac therapies, early diagnosis, and preventive strategies to combat the growing burden of heart diseases and hypertension in India.

press_release_title: Digital BP monitor is handy but not accurate. Know right way to use it
press_abstract: People at homes also are mostly using digital BP monitors nowadays. LUCKNOW: With mercury-based blood pressure (BP) monitors set to be phased out by 2025, soon people will be left with no other option but to use their digital counterparts. While government district hospitals are already using them compulsorily, people at homes also are mostly using digital BP monitors nowadays. Experts, however, are worried over accuracy of the results these devices give, especially when they are not used as per the correct procedure. "Hypertension patients need to monitor BP. Equally important, however, is to record correct reading. And for that people must be aware of dos and don'ts of using digital monitors at home," said Prof Pravesh Vishwakarma, facu cardiology department, KGMU. The sphygmomanometer, the gold standard for BP monitoring, was invented in 1896 by Scipione Riva-Rocci. However, as mercury is harmful to the atmosphere, the Minamata Convention decided to reduce and end the use of mercury-based instruments by 2025. India is among the 140 signatory countries. Though there is a 5-10% error when digital monitors are compared to mercury-based monitors, this difference can increase to 30% if not used properly. Emphasizing on the importance of measuring blood pressure for early diagnosis of health issues, Prof Vishwakarma said that the readings taken at home are more accurate than those taken in a clinical setting as patients may feel anxious in the latter situation. Digital BP monitors have several advantages over mercury devices, including being easy to use, and free of mercury. However, factors such as physical activity, stress, anxiety, and sitting posture can affect BP readings. Hence, it is necessary to take them into account while measuring BP, according to Vishwakarma. Prof Nar Singh Verma, from the physiology department at KGMU, advised patients to take BP measurements three times, with a minute's gap in each hand. If the reading fluctuates less than 10%, it is considered accurate. Former cardiologist at Balrampur Hospital Dr Praveen Sharma emphasized that the position of the cuff and tightness can also affect BP readings. Patients should wrap cuffs in the arm at the level of the chest neither too tight nor too loose. Consider these factors when taking their readings. Professor Bhuwan Tiwari, head of cardiology at RMLIMS, recommended that patients only buy digital BP devices with ISI certification. He also advised users to read the user's manual before using the device and to check the batteries regularly.

press_release_title: Cardiologists use TTE to screen CAD patients for abdominal aortic aneurysms
press_abstract: Cardiologists can provide additional value by screening patients with significant coronary artery disease (CAD) for the presence of abdominal aortic aneurysms (AAA), according to new research published in Cardiology Research. “The overall mortality of ruptured AAA is 80-90% compared with a 30-day postoperative mortality of 3% after elective surgery,” wrote first author Pravesh Vishwakarma, a cardiologist at King George’s Medical University in India, and colleagues. “Considering the huge impact of timely diagnosis and management, health organizations recommend at least one-time screening for AAA by ultrasonography for individuals aged between 65 and 75 years with history of smoking, thereby reducing AAA-related mortality rates by 50%.” Since most patients with significant CAD undergo a transthoracic echocardiography (TTE) anyway, Vishwakarma et al. noted that it might be an effective strategy to just perform AAA screening at that same time. To test such a strategy, the group tracked data from more than 500 CAD patients who were screened for AAA using TTE. Patients were treated at a single facility from Jan. 1 to Nov. 30, 2017. While 66.2% of patients were male, the mean patient age was 63 years old. Overall, the cardiologists observed the presence of an AAA in 4.8% of CAD patients. The mean size was “small” at 34 mm. Diabetes, peripheral artery disease and a family history of AAA were all confirmed as significant risk factors among the study’s patient population. Patients who presented with a history of smoking, hypertension or hyperlipidemia also faced a higher risk of AAA, but that difference was not statistically significant. Smoking is typically “strongly linked” with a higher risk of AAA, the authors explained. They suggested that their study did not find a significant association because 68.3% of participants were smokers. “We also found that TTE is as good as abdominal ultrasound for screening of AAA,” the authors added. “The use of TTE for patients with CAD to assess the abdominal aorta in the subcostal view was convenient and easy to perform.”

press_release_title: World Heart Day: TOI Medithon addresses the escalating concern of heart diseases
press_abstract: As per the World Health Organisation, heart diseases kill close to 17.9 million individuals every year and there is a need to address this silent killer. TOI Medithon, held on September 23, ahead of World Heart Day (September 29) addressed the escalating concern of cardiovascular diseases with eminent health experts, through the campaign "Treatment Zaroori Hai" by Meril. 1x1 polls Poll Do you exercise in the morning? Yes No The experts shared detailed insights about the reason behind the growing incidences of heart diseases, advanced technology and innovative methods used in treating cardiovascular diseases, and how to improve patient outcomes. In the discussion, Dr. Ashok Seth, Chairman, Fortis Escorts Heart Institute and Fortis Healthcare Medical Council said, "The prevalence of heart disease in India is the highest in the world. So, it is very important for us to understand how to prevent heart disease," while delivering the keynote address. "Every individual has to be responsible for prevention of heart disease and to bring action through regulators and policy makers or even our families or any sphere which we are a part of," Dr Seth added. Highlighting the objective of World Heart Day, Dr. Akhil Kumar Sharma, Prof. Department of Cardiology, King George's Medical University, Lucknow said, "World Heart Day, observed on September 29th, is a global initiative to raise awareness about cardiovascular diseases (CVDs) and promote heart health. Its importance lies in encouraging heart-healthy habits like exercise and a balanced diet, spreading knowledge about preventing heart disease early on, highlighting the increasing threat of heart disease, especially among young people, promoting education on risk factors like high blood pressure, stress, and smoking, empowering individuals to make informed lifestyle choices, supporting efforts to reduce healthcare costs through prevention, raising awareness about the latest advancements in heart disease management, and advocating for heart-friendly policies in workplaces and communities.” Experts shared their experiences, opinions and their recommendations on how to lead a healthy life and take care of the heart. There is a need to create more awareness around heart health as the incidence of the disease has migrated to younger individuals, thus making it even more alarming and unpredictable. The doctors have urged to follow a healthy lifestyle. Sponsored Links You May Like This technology can bring additional income for Indians AI Courses "As Indians or South Asians we are already predisposed to heart diseases," said Dr Biswaranjan Jena, Senior Consultant & Interventional Cardiologist, Dr. Jena's Cardio Diabetes Centre & Aswini Hospital, Cuttack. "Our lifestyle is not suitable, as we are mostly following Western lifestyle. Now the demographics are changing with many adopting smoking and drinking habits hence youngsters are getting heart diseases as well," he added. Dr. Kaushal Pandey, Senior Consultant Cardiac Surgeon, P D Hinduja Hospital and Medical Research Centre, Mumbai touched upon preventive care. "Prevention is so much better, cheaper and superior these days. If youngsters do 150 minutes of cardio in a week they can prevent heart diseases, this is not a lot of effort considering their health is at stake. My message to all is protect yourself by living a disciplined life," TOI Medithon also touched upon an important part of heart disease treatment which is advancement in technology. The integration of technological advancement and heart disease treatment is a saviour to mankind. Technology has not only made treatment easier, it has also shortened the recovery duration. “Technology has improvised cardiology to a great extent,” said Dr. Rajneesh Malhotra, Vice Chairman & Head- CTVS, Max Super Speciality Hospital, New Delhi. “Modern techniques have revolutionized cardiac care. For example, minimally invasive methods now use smaller incisions thanks to innovations like robotic-assisted surgery and keyhole surgeries for Cardiac surgery, said Dr. Rajneesh Malhotra. "In conventional surgeries the patient recovery time was longer, but thanks to technology they can go back to work within a few weeks," he added. Experts also spoke about TAVR and its impact on the future of heart valve treatment. Transcatheter Aortic Valve Replacement or TAVR is an advanced treatment option for common heart valve diseases like aortic valve stenosis. "At present, TAVR remains an innovative, cutting-edge treatment that many patients remain unaware of. Yet the benefits are immense for those who need it and cannot avail of surgical means of treatment," said Dr. Jaspal Arneja, Chief Cardiologist & Director Cath Lab, Arneja Heart Institute, Nagpur.

press_release_title: Ruckus in Gorakhpur AIIMS, Dr. Gaurav Gupta removed from CSSD; MPs will come to AIIMS today
press_abstract: Dr. Gaurav Gupta, Head of Surgery Department and Additional Professor at AIIMS Gorakhpur has been removed from the post of in-charge of Central Sterile Supply Department (CSSD). He has been given the responsibility of Fire Safety Committee. Dr. Gaurav Gupta has accused Executive Director (ED) and Chief Executive Officer (CEO) Prof. Gopal Krishna Pal of getting his son Dr. Auroprakash Pal admitted by getting a fake Other Backward Class (OBC) Non Creamy Layer (NCL) certificate made. Its investigation is still going on. On Wednesday, Sadar MP Ravi Kishan Shukla will reach AIIMS and will participate in many programs as well as hold a meeting with the seniors Allegations and counter-allegations have been going on in AIIMS Gorakhpur for several days. The ministry is keeping an eye on this. The certificate is also being investigated. ED Prof. Gopal Krishna Pal reached AIIMS at around 8 pm on Tuesday night. He immediately held a meeting with Deputy Director Administration Arun Singh and other senior officials in his office. In the meeting, it was decided to form four committees to improve the system. Many other issues were also discussed. Medical superintendent formed a committee Medical Superintendent Prof. Ajay Bharti formed four committees for better management in the campus. These include Diet and Kitchen Committee, CSSD Committee, Laundry and Linen Committee and Fire Safety Committee. Diet and Kitchen Committee will keep an eye on the food. Recently, after the news was published in Dainik Jagran, the cafeteria and four kitchens operating in AIIMS campus were closed. Expired and contaminated material was found being sold everywhere. Till now Dr. Gaurav Gupta was in charge of CSSD. Now a committee has been formed and another doctor of surgery has been given the responsibility. On Wednesday afternoon, Microbiology department head Dr. Atul, Dr. Rajnandan of orthopedics, Dr. Ravi Gupta of surgery etc. had inspected the operation theatre arrangements. They also noted the deficiencies. Information has also been received about many equipments being faulty. All these equipments are sent to CSSD after the operation. MPs will arrive at 3 pm in the afternoon MP Ravi Kishan will reach AIIMS campus on Wednesday at 3 pm. He will participate in the campaign 'Ek Ped in Maa Ke Naam' and will clean the campus under the Swachhata Hi Seva program. After this, he will hold a meeting with the institute's seniors. The MP will discuss the chaos that has come to light in AIIMS in recent days as well as the treatment and teaching environment. There will also be a discussion on increasing the number of operation theatres. At present, only four operation theatres are operational in AIIMS, due to which patients have to wait for a long time for operations. These are the functions of CSSD CSSD includes cleaning, packing, disinfection, sterilization of operation related equipment. On Wednesday, the term CSSD was first used by the American College of Surgeons in the year 1928. In the United Kingdom, CSSD was developed by the Cambridge Military Hospital in the year 1955. In India, the first CSSD was established in the year 1965 at Safdarjung Hospital, New Delhi. The policeman spoke to Dr. Gaurav Gupta Gorakhpur: AIIMS police has become swift on the complaint of unreasonable settlement of the complaint made on the Jansunwai portal on September 5. A policeman from AIIMS police station spoke to Dr. Gaurav Gupta on Tuesday and recorded the information related to the complaint. On September 15, AIIMS police had disposed of Dr. Gaurav Gupta's complaint citing ongoing investigation. Dr. Gaurav Gupta said that no policeman talked to him and filed a report of disposal. He gave a bad feedback of disposal on the Jansunwai portal. After this, the investigation has started again.

press_release_title: Infant doing well after complicated cardiac op at BHU
press_abstract: Doctors of the Institute of Medical Sciences performed a PDA (patent ductus arteriosus) device closure using transcatheter method in a baby weighing 5 kg. The doctors claimed the case was a significant achievement for cardiology department at IMS BHU, as this was the youngest child to undergo such an intervention here. The patient was discharged and is currently doing well. The team of doctors included Prof Vikas Agrawal, Dr Pratibha Rai, Dr Manish, Dr Arjun from the Cardiology department, and Prof A P Singh, Dr Sanjeev, and Dr Pratima from Cardiac Anaesthesia. Dr Pratibha Rai explained that the PDA is a structure that connects two major arteries in the body and is critical for fetal survival. However, if it does not close on its own after birth, it can lead to poor weight gain, heart failure and repeated episodes of pneumonia. In this case, the baby was six and half months old but not gaining weight. press_release_title: BHU hospital doctors successfully perform first TAVI procedure on 65-year-old patient
press_abstract: A team of skilled doctors at the Department of Cardiology at the Institute of Medical Sciences, Banaras Hindu University, successfully performed a TAVI (Transcatheter Aortic Valve Implantation) on a 65-year-old male suffering from a bicuspid aortic valve with severe stenosis. This case marks a significant achievement in structural interventional cardiology. The patient is discharged now and is currently doing well. TAVI, also known as TAVR (Transcatheter Aortic Valve Replacement), is a catheter-based procedure used to replace a diseased aortic valve without open-heart surgery. It is primarily used to treat severe aortic stenosis, especially in patients who are at high or intermediate surgical risk. The procedure is performed via a catheter, usually inserted through the femoral artery (transfemoral approach) or other alternative access points (subclavian, transapical, or direct aortic). A bioprosthetic valve is delivered and expanded inside the diseased aortic valve. Since it avoids open-heart surgery, patients usually have shorter hospital stays and quicker recovery times. The team was led by Prof Vikas Agrawal, Dr Pratibha Rai, and Dr Suyash Tripathi from the Cardiology Department, along with Prof A P Singh, Dr Sanjeev, and Dr Pratima from Cardiac Anaesthesia, and Prof Siddartha Lakhotia from the Cardiothoracic and Vascular Surgeons Department. The doctors said that the aortic valve is a left-sided valve of the heart that guards the aorta, which supplies oxygenated blood to the body. In case of obstruction to the aortic valve, the patient will have a limited supply of blood to the body and presents with chest pain, fatigue, and syncope. The aortic valve obstruction can be congenital, called a bicuspid aortic valve, and once diagnosed, should be followed up regularly since childhood. A bicuspid aortic valve is the most common congenital heart disease (1-2%) and can have varied presentations from asymptomatic to severely symptomatic aortic stenosis and aortic regurgitation. Acquired old age-related sclerosis without timely intervention carries a very high mortality. Traditionally, surgical repair was the primary treatment, but it came with considerable risks, particularly in elderly patients with comorbidities. Prof Vikas Agrawal underscored the technical difficulties of TAVI in elderly patients with multiple comorbiditiesHe highlighted that advancements in structural interventional techniques now make it possible to repair these defects with minimally invasive procedures, providing a safer alternative to open-heart surgery for high-risk individuals. Prof Siddharth Lakhotia, Head of CTVS, noted that while open-heart surgery remains an option, its high mortality rate often makes transcatheter valve implantation a more favourable choice in carefully selected patients.

press_release_title: BHU doctors save 10-yr-old girl with high-risk heart procedure
press_abstract: A successful high-risk balloon pulmonary valvuloplasty was performed on a 10-year-old girl with severe pulmonary stenosis in the department of cardiology at Institute of Medical Sciences (IMS), BHU on Tuesday. The procedure was carried out by a team comprising Dr Dharmendra Jain, Dr Pratibha Rai, Dr Srishti, and Dr Arjun. The procedure was done under the Chief Minister/Prime Minister's Relief Fund. Dr Pratibha said that the case was particularly challenging due to the patient's critical condition, with oxygen saturation at 80% and severe right ventricular dysfunction. Post-procedure, the patient was stable and discharged after two days. Since the joining of Dr Pratibha Rai, a paediatric cardiologist, last month, the department of cardiology has treated around 10 patients with congenital heart diseases. The head of the department, Prof Vikas Agrawal, praised the team's efforts and announced that paediatric cardiology procedures will now be reqularly available at IMS, BHU. Prof Siddharth Lakhotia, head of cardiac surgery, expressed great happiness at the start of this much-needed facility and said that children who previously required open-heart surgery for their heart defects can now be treated without any incision.

press_release_title: Over 2,000 affected: Of 15 in ICU, 2 are on ventilator
press_abstract: : Of the 62,000 participants at Mumbai Marathon, 2,018 needed medical help, with 26 requiring hospitalisation. Until Sunday evening, 16 were discharged. No fatality was reported until press time, in 2024 two deaths had occurred. Dr Santosh Kumar Dora, consultant cardiologist at Asian Heart Institute and medical director of Tata Mumbai Marathon 2025, said that most participants who required medical attention suffered minor issues such as muscle cramps, leg pain, minor bruises, and dehydration. His institute set up 15 aid stations, three base camps, and deployed 600 volunteers, including doctors, nurses, and physiotherapists. Fifteen of the hospitalised participants were admitted to Bombay Hospital, the closest medical facility to the event arena. press_release_title: Winter and Heart Health: How to Recognize Early Signs of a Heart Attack in Winters
press_abstract: Winter is a great season to enjoy chilling temperatures and go for fun activities, but it also comes with its own set of health risks, particularly when it comes to heart health. Cold temperatures can strain the cardiovascular system. Understanding how winter affects your heart and recognizing the early signs of a heart problem is crucial for taking prompt action and making lifestyle changes. How Winter Affects Your Heart Health Winter can significantly affect heart health, as the cold weather causes blood vessels to constrict, making the heart work harder to pump blood. This increased workload raises both heart rate and blood pressure for individuals with pre-existing heart conditions, which can lead to severe complications such as heart attacks. Additionally, during the colder months, people often reduce physical activity, leading to poor blood circulation. Moreover, the temptation to indulge in rich, fatty foods can raise cholesterol levels, all of which contribute to increased strain on the cardiovascular system. It's important to stay informed and know the early signs of a heart attack, especially during winter. One of the most common early warning signs of heart attack is chest pain or discomfort, which may feel like pressure, tightness, or squeezing. This discomfort can last for a few minutes or may come and go, and during winter, it could easily be mistaken for muscle aches. Moreover, shortness of breath without exertion can also be a red flag, especially if it occurs alongside chest pain or on its own. People with heart issues might notice cold sweats, especially when they occur without physical activity, which is another critical symptom, and anyone experiencing this should seek immediate medical help. Additionally, extreme fatigue or weakness may indicate that the heart is not pumping effectively, which can be exacerbated by the colder months when people are less active. Heart attack pain can also radiate to the neck, jaw, or back or down one or both arms, signalling that blood flow to the heart is insufficient. Lastly, sudden dizziness, light-headedness, fainting, and chest discomfort or shortness of breath might be an emergency. Recognizing these signs early is imperative to ensuring timely medical intervention and reducing the risk of life-threatening complications. During the winter months, we tend to spend more time indoors and perform less physical activity. This can lead to weight gain, increased cholesterol levels, and higher blood pressure, all of which contribute to heart problems. Additionally, cold weather itself causes blood vessels to narrow, raising blood pressure and increasing strain on the heart. For individuals with pre-existing heart conditions or those who are older, these factors heighten the risk of heart attack. To reduce the risk of heart attacks during the winter months, it is important to follow certain preventive measures, such as avoiding sudden exposure to extremely cold temperatures. Layer up with warm clothes, hats, gloves, and scarves to help maintain body warmth and avoid shock to the heart. While it may be tempting to hibernate during the cold months, staying active is essential for heart health. Even light exercises like walking inside the home or doing simple stretches can keep your blood flowing and maintain a healthy heart. They must avoid fatty, high-cholesterol foods and focus on heart-healthy options like fruits, vegetables, lean proteins, and whole grains. This will help reduce the strain on your heart. Winter poses specific challenges to heart health, and cold weather can increase the risk of heart attacks. By understanding the risks and recognizing the early signs of a heart attack, one can take the necessary steps to protect the heart.

press_release_title: Managing Hypertension During Winter in India: Insights from Dr. Asit Khanna at Yashoda Hospital, Ghaziabad
press_abstract: Managing Hypertension During Winter in India: Insights from Dr. Asit Khanna at Yashoda Hospital, Ghaziabad Hypertension, or high blood pressure, is a major health concern in India, affecting a large segment of the population. The winter season poses additional challenges as colder temperatures can exacerbate the condition, especially among those with pre-existing cardiovascular issues. Dr. Asit Khanna, one of the best cardiologists in Ghaziabad, provides key insights and guidance on managing hypertension during the colder months. The Indian Context: Hypertension in Winter In India, winter varies across regions, with northern states experiencing harsh cold while southern areas remain moderately cool. Despite regional differences, winter poses common challenges to managing hypertension: Cold Weather and Vasoconstriction: Lower temperatures lead to narrowing of blood vessels (vasoconstriction), increasing blood pressure. Dietary Preferences: Indian diets during winter often include rich, spicy, and calorie-dense foods that can aggravate hypertension. Reduced Physical Activity: : Shorter days and chilly weather discourage outdoor activities, leading to sedentary lifestyles. Stress Levels: Winter festivals like Christmas and New Year, while joyous, often come with overindulgence and stress that can trigger blood pressure spikes. The Importance of Cultural and Regional Factors Understanding the Indian lifestyle is crucial in tailoring hypertension management. For instance: Traditional Indian Foods: High salt intake in pickles, papads, and winter comfort foods like ghee-rich parathas can impact blood pressure. Rural vs. Urban Settings:Urban residents often lead sedentary lives, whereas rural populations may struggle with access to healthcare during extreme Tips for Managing Hypertension in Indian Winters 1. Dietary Adjustments Indian cuisine can be adapted to promote heart health: Reduce Salt Intake: Avoid excessive use of table salt, pickles, and processed foods. Embrace Seasonal Fruits and Vegetables:Winter vegetables like spinach (palak), carrots, and radishes are rich in nutrients and help control blood pressure. Heart-Friendly Spices:: Incorporate turmeric, garlic, and fenugreek seeds into meals for their antihypertensive properties. Healthy Alternatives:Replace refined flour with whole grains like bajra (pearl millet) and jowar (sorghum), which are traditional Indian winter staples. Manage stress through: Mindfulness Practices: Meditation and deep-breathing techniques can reduce anxiety-induced blood pressure spikes. Moderation:Avoid overindulgence in festive foods and alcohol. Limit Alcohol and Smoking Culturally, alcohol consumption may increase during festive gatherings. Limit intake, as both alcohol and smoking can worsen hypertension. press_release_title: TOI Medithon: Experts shed light on how to prevent, manage heart diseases and have 'Amazing Heart, Amazing Life'
press_abstract: Statistics on heart diseases and its rate of incidence have always been alarming, yet every year the number of cases and the deaths associated with the disease increases. While several initiatives have been undertaken to narrow down the factors that increase the risk to cardiovascular health, a lot of gaps still need to be filled. Be it the access to the right kind of information or the availability of hospital care, cardiovascular health continues to be heavily impacted. On World Heart Day, TOI Medithon hosted eminent health experts who shared valuable insights on strategies to prevent heart diseases, tips to manage them, the alarming rise of heart disease among young people, and how women are affected. First session: "Strategies to Prevent Heart Diseases: Know Your Numbers Amazing Heart, Amazing Life with Expert Insights" The crux of heart disease prevention strategies lies in reducing the risk of life-threatening conditions such as heart attacks and strokes. By adopting healthy habits like a balanced diet and regular exercise, individuals can improve cardiovascular function, lower cholesterol, and maintain healthy blood pressure levels. "We must realise heart disease is a lifestyle related disease and we should also know that it can be prevented," said Dr Balbir Singh, Chairman & Head, PAN Max Cardiology Max Superspeciality Hospital, New Delhi, and emphasized on practicing yoga and meditation and embracing the rich ancient Indian tradition. Physical activity, coupled with mindfulness have been strongly linked to a better quality of life and a good heart. The impact of stress on heart health is not unknown to us and on this Dr Davinder Singh Chadha, Senior Consultant & Interventional Cardiologist, Manipal Hospital, & Sri Jaydeva Institute of Cardiovascular Sciences & Research, Bengaluru, shared some valuable insights. "Stress is a part and parcel of life," said Dr Chadha about the inseparable aspect of life these days. "Always share your inner feelings because stress eventually changes the internal harmony and releases hormones which can rupture the blocked artery causing heart attack," he recommended and stressed upon the need of sleep. Dr Pramod Kumar Kuchalakanti, HOD Cardiology, and Dir. Cardiac Catheterization Lab, Yashoda Hospitals, Hyderabad talked about the effect of obesity on heart health. "Obesity stems from diet, lack of exercise," he emphasized on the silent epidemic that is affecting almost every other individual. He highlighted a trend that is going viral these days; he said that even when you go for advanced treatment like bariatric surgery to lose weight, it might come back. Second session: "Managing Heart Diseases: Amazing Heart, Amazing Life with Expert Insights". Managing heart disease requires a combination of lifestyle changes, medications, and regular medical care. "Coronary artery disease is a number one killer," Dr Vinod Vijain, Owner & Dir. of Vijan Hospital and Research Centre Nashik, said and added that in order to manage heart diseases one needs to improve the lipid profile, maintain a healthy lifestyle and take medicines consistently. A heart-healthy diet low in salt, saturated fats, and refined sugars helps control cholesterol and blood pressure. Regular physical activity improves heart function and circulation. Quitting smoking and limiting alcohol intake are essential to prevent further damage. Following this, Dr Vivekananda Gajapati, Chief consultant cardiologist in the Department of Cardiology at SDM Narayana Heart Centre, Dharwad, Karnataka spoke on the risk factors that worsen heart health. "The traditional risk factors for heart disease are hypertension, dyslipidemia, smoking, obesity and sedentary lifestyle, " said Dr Vivekanand and stressed on hypertension. He said hypertension at a young age worsens heart health. He recommends timely lipid profile assessment after the age of 20. While both the experts talked about management of heart diseases and its risk factors, Dr Pramod Mundra, Senior Interventional Cardiologist and Dir. Platina Heart Hospital, Nagpur touched upon a vital aspect of the disease. He talked about the symptoms of heart attack. "Heaviness in the centre of the chest is a primary symptom," he said and advised not to panic and call for help as soon as possible. Third Session: "Heart Diseases and Youngsters: Amazing Heart, Amazing Life with Expert Insights". Heart disease is increasingly affecting young people due to lifestyle factors such as poor diet, lack of physical activity, smoking, and stress. Unhealthy eating habits, high in processed foods and sugars, contribute to obesity, high cholesterol, and hypertension, all risk factors for heart disease. Sedentary lifestyles, coupled with excessive screen time, reduce cardiovascular fitness. Shedding more light on how stress is killing young lives, Dr Rajneesh Kapoor, Chairman, Interventional Cardiology & Cardiac Care Management MEDANTA THE MEDI CITY, Gurgaon spoke on work related stress. "Individuals at a young age are showing the symptoms of heart attack. The balance is somewhere disturbed in everyone's life these days," he said. "Youngsters need to understand that there is a need to have balance," he added and stressed on erratic lifestyle habits like consuming fried foods, smoking, alcohol, etc. "Be ambitious, think about your growth, but don't be overambitious," he sent a message to the youngsters. In the same line, Dr Arun Chopra, Dir. Cardiology Fortis Escorts Hospital Amritsar said, "if you want to have good health, you need to have the right attitude. Adopt holistic change, healthy lifestyle including diet, exercise, stress management and good sleep." Heart disease is a leading cause of death among women, yet it is often under-recognized. Women may experience different symptoms than men, such as shortness of breath, nausea, or back pain, which can delay diagnosis. Hormonal changes, especially after menopause, increase the risk of heart disease as estrogen levels drop, affecting heart and blood vessel health. On the lack of awareness around heart diseases in women, Dr Arun Gopi, Dir. Cath Lab Head Dept Of Electrophysiology Metromed International Cardiac Centre Calicut, Kerala said, "heart disease was earlier considered to be a disease of the men. It is the leading cause of mortality in females as well. Women should have the right knowledge around the symptoms, understanding the risk factors so that they don't get fooled." "One of the typical signs of heart attack is angina. It increases more on exertion and can be associated with nausea and other signs. The problem with women is that they don't have the typical symptoms. So, the message is, it doesn't matter what the intensity of the pain is, if a woman complains of chest pain, get her to a physician immediately, said Dr Kunal Mahajan, Dir. and Chief Interventional Cardiologist, Himachal Heart Institute, Mandi, Himachal Pradesh. "In women there is lack of awareness and there will also be lack of self importance. The tragedy is that in India there has been an increase of 300% of heart attack cases among women in the last 2-3 decades," said Dr Asit Khanna, Principal Cardiologist and Cath Lab. Dir Yashoda Super Speciality Hospital, Kaushambi.

press_release_title: Structural heart disease – An overview
press_abstract: Structural heart disease – an overview Structural heart disease is a term used to describe problems with the heart’s structure, most commonly its valves. The defects in the heart could be congenital (present at the time of birth) or may be developed in adulthood. As a result of aging, injury, and infections. Structural diseases need to be treated as the soonest. If left untreated they may result in various life-threatening heart complications that may lead to irrecoverable damage. Dr. Asit Khanna is the best interventional cardiologist in Ghaziabad. He has performed many catheter-based procedures and is a highly skilled cardiologist for the treatment of heart problems. Statistically, more than 10% of adults over the age of 65 have some structural heart disease, it could be a defect in the heart’s valves, walls, or chambers. The most common type of structural heart disease is: Aortic valve stenosis: When the heart’s aortic valve narrows, aortic stenosis occurs. The heart’s valve doesn’t fully open thereby blocking blood flow to the main artery and the rest of the body. The most common cause of aortic stenosis is an accumulation of calcium deposits, that increases with age. Severe aortic stenosis can be very dangerous. The treatment of aortic stenosis requires open-heart surgery to replace the damaged valve. Dr. Asit Khanna is highly skilled at performing open-heart surgery. Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy: The heart muscle becomes abnormally thick, making it hard for the heart to pump blood. With proper treatment, damage can be slowed and the symptoms can be lessened. Atrial septal defect: It is the most common type of congenital heart defect. It is characterized by the presence of a hole in the heart, in between the upper chambers. Open-heart surgery is the best treatment for atrial septal defects. Mitral valve prolapses: It can be developed at any age. It affects the left chambers of the heart. It could be genetic and is linked to infections of the connective tissue. Irregular heartbeat, palpitations, and shortness of breath are common symptoms. Myocarditis: The heart muscle is inflamed and the inflammation leads to reduced ability of the heart to pump blood efficiently. It can cause arrhythmias. Sometimes viral infections also cause myocarditis. Symptoms include tightness and squeezing pain in the chest. The tests to confirm structural heart defects include MRI, ECG, or a chest X-ray.

press_release_title: Advancements in non-invasive diagnostic techniques for heart disease
press_abstract: Advancements in non-invasive diagnostic techniques for heart disease Heart disease is a leading cause of death worldwide, with an estimated 17.9 million deaths attributed to cardiovascular disease each year. Early diagnosis and treatment are essential in reducing the risk of complications and improving patient outcomes. However, traditional diagnostic techniques for heart disease, such as cardiac catheterization, can be invasive and carry risks for patients. Fortunately, advancements in non-invasive diagnostic techniques have made it possible to accurately diagnose heart disease without the need for invasive procedures. One of the most significant advancements in non-invasive diagnostic techniques for heart disease is the use of computed tomography (CT) scans. CT scans use X-rays to produce detailed images of the heart and surrounding structures. The technology has improved significantly in recent years, with newer scanners offering faster scan times and higher resolution images. CT scans are particularly useful in diagnosing coronary artery disease, a condition in which the arteries that supply blood to the heart become narrowed or blocked. By detecting blockages in the coronary arteries, CT scans can help identify patients who may benefit from interventions such as angioplasty or bypass surgery. CT scans can also help diagnose other heart conditions, such as aortic aneurysms and pulmonary embolisms. Another non-invasive diagnostic technique that has improved in recent years is magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). MRI uses a powerful magnetic field and radio waves to produce detailed images of the heart and blood vessels. Unlike CT scans, MRI does not use ionizing radiation, which makes it a safer option for patients. MRI is particularly useful in diagnosing heart muscle diseases such as cardiomyopathy and heart failure. It can also help detect problems with the heart's valves and blood vessels. Other non-invasive diagnostic techniques for heart disease include echocardiography, which uses sound waves to produce images of the heart, and nuclear imaging, which uses a small amount of radioactive material to create images of the heart. The benefits of non-invasive diagnostic techniques for heart disease are clear. They are safer, more comfortable, and less invasive for patients than traditional diagnostic techniques. They are also more accurate, allowing physicians to diagnose heart disease earlier and with greater confidence. In addition, non-invasive diagnostic techniques have reduced the need for invasive procedures like cardiac catheterization, which carry risks such as bleeding, infection, and damage to blood vessels. By reducing the number of invasive procedures, non-invasive diagnostic techniques can also help reduce healthcare costs. Despite the benefits of non-invasive diagnostic techniques for heart disease, there are some limitations to their use. For example, CT scans and MRI can be expensive and may not be readily available in all healthcare settings. In addition, some patients may not be able to undergo these tests due to contraindications such as claustrophobia or metal implants. advancements in non-invasive diagnostic techniques for heart disease have been a significant development in modern medicine. The use of CT scans, MRI, and other non-invasive techniques has made it possible to accurately diagnose heart disease without the need for invasive procedures. These techniques are safer, more comfortable, and more accurate than traditional diagnostic techniques, and they have the potential to improve patient outcomes and reduce healthcare costs. While there are some limitations to their use, the benefits of non-invasive diagnostic techniques for heart disease cannot be overstated.
